title: Trump announces sweeping 'Liberation Day' tariffs on nearly all US trading partners
full_text: President Trump on Wednesday announced a sweeping new tariff regime he dubbed 'Liberation Day,' imposing a baseline 10% tariff on all imports into the United States, with significantly higher rates targeting key trading partners. China faces a 34% tariff, the European Union 20%, and Japan 24%. The announcement, made from the White House Rose Garden, represents the most aggressive trade action taken by any US administration in decades. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said the tariffs would take effect immediately for the baseline rate, with the higher country-specific rates kicking in after a short phase-in period. Markets reacted sharply, with the US Dollar Index (DXY) falling 1.2% as investors weighed the risk of retaliatory measures and a potential global trade war. Economists warned the move could reignite inflation pressures and complicate the Federal Reserve's path on interest rates. The S&P 500 futures dropped over 3% in after-hours trading.

title: Fed's Powell says central bank is 'well positioned' to wait on rate cuts amid tariff uncertainty
full_text: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said Friday the central bank is in no hurry to adjust interest rates as it monitors the economic fallout from the Trump administration's new tariff regime. Speaking at a conference in Arlington, Virginia, Powell said the Fed is 'well positioned to wait for greater clarity' before making any moves on monetary policy. He acknowledged that the tariffs, if sustained, could push inflation higher while simultaneously weighing on growth — a stagflationary dynamic that would complicate the Fed's dual mandate. Powell's comments were interpreted by markets as a signal that rate cuts expected for mid-2025 may be pushed back. Fed funds futures shifted after his remarks, with traders now pricing in only one cut by year-end versus two cuts priced before the speech. The DXY initially rallied on the hawkish tilt before giving back gains as recession fears dominated sentiment.

title: China vows 'fight to the end' as Beijing announces 34% retaliatory tariffs on all US goods
full_text: China's Ministry of Commerce announced Friday it would impose 34% retaliatory tariffs on all US imports effective April 10th, matching the rate imposed by Washington on Chinese goods under President Trump's Liberation Day order. A ministry spokesman said China would 'fight to the end' if the US continued to escalate. The announcement sent shockwaves through global markets, with the offshore yuan weakening sharply against the dollar and Asian equity markets posting steep losses. The euro and yen both strengthened against the dollar as investors fled to perceived safe havens within the G10 currency space. Analysts at Goldman Sachs warned that a full-scale US-China trade war could shave 1.5 percentage points off US GDP growth in 2025. The DXY dropped 0.9% on the session as risk-off flows paradoxically weighed on the dollar amid fears of a US growth shock.

title: Wall Street strategists divided on dollar outlook as tariff shock reshapes FX landscape
full_text: Currency strategists across major Wall Street banks are sharply divided on the near-term outlook for the US dollar following the Trump administration's sweeping tariff announcements this week. JPMorgan's FX team argues the dollar should weaken further as tariffs act as a growth tax on the US economy, eroding the rate differential advantage that has supported DXY since 2022. Goldman Sachs takes the opposing view, contending that tariff-driven inflation will keep the Fed on hold longer than peers, sustaining real yield support for the dollar. Deutsche Bank sits in the middle, flagging that the dollar's direction will hinge on whether trading partners retaliate aggressively or seek negotiation. The divergence in views reflects genuine uncertainty about which channel — growth shock or inflation persistence — dominates the dollar's reaction function in a tariff regime. Options markets show elevated implied volatility on DXY, suggesting traders are pricing in a wide range of outcomes over the next 30 days.

title: US ISM Services PMI falls to 50.8 in March, missing estimates of 52.5
full_text: The Institute for Supply Management's services sector index fell to 50.8 in March from 53.5 in February, coming in well below the consensus economist estimate of 52.5, data released Thursday showed. The reading, while still above the 50-point threshold separating expansion from contraction, marked the weakest print since mid-2024 and raised concerns about softening domestic demand ahead of the tariff shock. The new orders sub-index dropped sharply to 50.4 from 52.2, while the employment component slipped below 50 for the first time since late 2023. The prices paid index rose to 60.9, signalling persistent services inflation even as activity slows — adding to stagflation concerns. The dollar weakened modestly on the release as traders interpreted the miss as increasing the probability of Fed easing later in the year, before paring losses as risk-off sentiment from tariff headlines reasserted itself.
